title: Nine Nations Back Ukraine's Freyja Interceptor, US Ammo Production Stalls, NATO Picks Saab Over Boeing for AWACS Replacement
subhead: Nine European nations formally joined Ukraine's Freyja anti-ballistic missile coalition with a one-year deployment target, a DoD Inspector General report revealed the Army's 155-mm shell production is stuck at 36% of its goal, and NATO chose Saab's GlobalEye over Boeing's E-7 Wedgetail for its next radar plane — a $4.5B decision shaped by Washington's own budget whiplash.

sections.heading: Freyja Coalition Goes Official: Nine Nations, One Year Target
sections.body:
Nine European countries — Denmark, France, Germany, Italy, the Netherlands, Norway, Spain, Sweden, and the United Kingdom — formally signed on as founding members of Ukraine's Integrated Anti-Ballistic Missile Coalition at a meeting in Paris on Monday, President Volodymyr Zelenskyy announced. The coalition centers on the Freyja interceptor system, built around Ukrainian firm Fire Point's FP-7.X interceptor, designed to hit ballistic targets at roughly 15 miles altitude.

France's President Emmanuel Macron also announced a bilateral roadmap with Ukraine covering 16 Rafale fighter jets with accompanying weapon systems, SAMP/T NG air-defense batteries, radar systems, and licensing agreements for Ukraine to locally produce AASM glide-bomb kits, Aster 30 interceptors, and SCALP/Storm Shadow cruise missiles. The first Rafales are expected in Ukrainian skies by 2028 or 2029.

The Freyja coalition explicitly targets a 12-month operational timeline — a stark contrast to Patriot production, which takes 24 months per interceptor. Ukraine will supply the interceptor; partners will provide radar, tracking, and command-and-control systems. Major defense firms including MBDA, Thales, Leonardo, Saab, Diehl Defence, and Kongsberg attended the meeting, according to Zelenskyy.

The initiative was launched in response to a global shortage of anti-ballistic interceptors, worsened by the Iran war's demand drain. As Zelenskyy put it: 'The threat of ballistic missiles in the world is only going to increase. Therefore, Freyja must become a reality.'
sections.whyItMatters: Freyja is the most concrete European attempt yet to build an independent anti-ballistic capability that doesn't rely on US Patriot production lines. If it hits the 12-month target, it would represent a dramatic acceleration of defense procurement norms — and give Europe its own answer to a capability gap that the Iran war has made painfully visible.

sections.heading: US 155-mm Shell Production Stuck at 36,000 Per Month — Far Below 100K Goal
sections.body:
A Department of Defense Inspector General report published this month found that the Army's 155-mm artillery shell production reached only 36,000 rounds per month as of March 2026 — well short of the congressionally mandated goal of 100,000 rounds per month by October 2025.

The bottleneck is the Universal Artillery Projectile Lines facility in Mesquite, Texas, a $469 million investment operated by General Dynamics Ordnance and Tactical Systems that opened in 2024. The IG report found that the contractor has been unable to produce any projectile metal parts that meet contract specifications. The factory tried to adapt M107-era (1958) production equipment for the newer M795 shell variant — a shortcut that didn't work. Army officials also raised concerns that the Mesquite contract was never opened to competition.

The shortfall leaves three existing plants in Scranton and Wilkes-Barre, Pennsylvania, and Ingersoll, Canada carrying the load. Production has nearly tripled from 14,000 to 36,000 rounds monthly, and the Army hopes to reach 140,000 rounds per month by December 2027 through modernization of the Iowa Army Ammunition Plant and new facilities in Kansas and Arkansas. The IG recommended reviewing the Mesquite contract for potential fund recovery.
sections.whyItMatters: This is the most detailed official accounting yet of why the US defense industrial base can't scale artillery production — and it's a $469 million case study in how not to do it. The Mesquite failure illustrates the gap between budget commitments and manufacturing reality, and the Iran war's ongoing munitions demand makes closing that gap urgent.

sections.heading: NATO Picks Saab GlobalEye Over Boeing E-7 for AWACS Replacement
sections.body:
NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte announced at the alliance's Defence Industry Forum in Ankara that 11 allies — Belgium, Canada, Denmark, Germany, Latvia, Lithuania, Luxembourg, the Netherlands, Norway, Romania, and Sweden — will procure up to 10 Saab GlobalEye aircraft to replace the aging E-3 AWACS fleet. Saab CEO Micael Johansson estimated the deal at around $4.5 billion, with deliveries beginning around 2030.

The decision snubs Boeing's E-7 Wedgetail, which NATO had originally planned to buy in 2023. The reversal was driven in part by the Pentagon's own budget whiplash: when the FY27 budget initially included no funding for E-7 procurement, NATO allies took it as a signal that Washington wasn't committed. Though Secretary Hegseth later reversed that decision and House appropriators backed a $1.5 billion E-7 budget amendment, the damage was done. Analyst J.J. Gertler of the Teal Group called it an 'own goal.'

The GlobalEye uses sensors on a Bombardier Global 6000/6500 airframe to detect drones, ballistic missiles, and hypersonic threats. Saab noted that no contract has been signed yet. Boeing said it remains 'fully committed' to the E-7 and highlighted its 'active production line' and interoperability with allied systems.
sections.whyItMatters: NATO's AWACS decision is the clearest signal yet that European allies are willing to buy European when Washington sends mixed signals on its own procurements. The $4.5 billion Saab deal also represents a significant competitive win for European defense industry over a US titan — and a direct consequence of US budget instability.

sections.heading: Navy Issues RFI for Carrier-Based Drone Family of Systems
sections.body:
The US Navy issued a Request for Information on July 14 for its 'Air Wing of the Future Family of Systems,' seeking industry input on a family of carrier-based autonomous platforms capable of performing eight missions: anti-ship strikes, land attack, anti-submarine warfare, air-to-air combat, electronic warfare, ISR, aerial refueling, and resupply.

The RFI specifies a minimum 1,000-nautical mile range for strike missions and requires both flight autonomy (carrier pattern, taxiing) and mission autonomy (dynamic retasking, threat evasion, automated aerial refueling). Notably, the Navy also wants vertical-takeoff UAVs that can operate from destroyers and mobile sea bases — not just carriers.

The drones must demonstrate 'increased combat effectiveness over current fourth-generation platforms at a given spot factor,' and contractors must explain how they'd achieve rapid surge scaling and keep unit costs sustainable. The response deadline is August 13.

This effort is part of the Trump administration's Golden Fleet expansion plan and complements existing programs like the MQ-25A Stingney tanker and the Collaborative Combat Aircraft (CCA). It signals the Navy's intention to shift carrier air wings toward a mix of manned and unmanned platforms built for attrition-tolerant, high-volume operations.
sections.whyItMatters: The RFI is the Navy's most detailed public articulation yet of what a post-Fourth-Gen carrier air wing looks like. Eight mission sets from a single family of systems is ambitious — and the requirement for VTOL variants operating from smaller vessels is a direct response to the distributed warfare concepts tested in the Iran conflict.

sections.heading: Pentagon Halts CMMC Phase 2, Launches 60-Day Reform Review
sections.body:
The Defense Department suspended Phase 2 of the Cybersecurity Maturity Model Certification (CMMC) program on Monday, canceling the third-party certification requirement that was set to begin November 10. DOD CIO Kirsten Davies announced a 60-day reform review to realign CMMC with Secretary Hegseth's acquisition priorities emphasizing speed and lower barriers for new entrants.

Phase 1 self-assessments remain in place. Phases 3 and 4 are also suspended. The Small Business Administration reported that CMMC compliance costs had driven some companies out of the defense industrial base entirely, delaying delivery of critical capabilities. Under Secretary Michael Duffey called the move a way to 'maintain a strict security baseline while removing paralyzing costs.'

A CMMC Reform Task Force will review which NIST 800-171 controls deliver meaningful risk reduction, whether commercial cybersecurity tools can substitute for separate assessments, and what's actually driving compliance costs. An RFI is open through August 14.
sections.whyItMatters: CMMC has been a flashpoint for years — small contractors called it a cost nightmare, and the Pentagon's own data showed it was shrinking the industrial base at exactly the wrong time. The suspension is a significant regulatory pivot that could reshape how 300,000+ defense suppliers handle cybersecurity compliance, but it also raises questions about what replaces third-party certification.

sections.heading: Iran War Supplemental: $87.6B Request Adds Complexity to FY27 Budget
sections.body:
The White House's $87.6 billion supplemental funding request for the Iran war and related expenses is compounding an already tangled FY27 defense budget process. The supplemental adds to a $1.1 trillion base budget request and a $350 billion reconciliation request, creating what analysts describe as a three-part budget puzzle for Congress to sort out.

The single largest item is $21 billion for munitions, to rebuild stockpiles depleted by months of combat operations. The FY27 request already sought $76.3 billion for munitions — 185% above FY26's $26.8 billion. Combined with the supplemental, the administration has now asked for $97.3 billion for munitions this year — more than three times the Army's entire FY26 procurement budget of $30.5 billion.

Other notable supplemental items include $17.3 billion for operational costs, $2.4 billion for drones (including replacement of two dozen MQ-9A Reapers lost in combat — though the MQ-9A is no longer in production), $4 billion for Airborne Moving Target Indication and Space Data Network (part of the Golden Dome initiative, with SpaceX having won a $2.3B SDN contract and a $4.2B AMTI contract in May), and $12.1 billion for classified programs.

Military leaders warned in May that without supplemental backfill, they would need to begin curtailing regular training and operations this summer.
sections.whyItMatters: The supplemental reveals the actual cost of the Iran war in procurement terms — $97.3 billion for munitions alone across FY27 is an extraordinary figure. The MQ-9A production gap also raises questions about what replaces it, and the $4.2B SpaceX AMTI contract signals how much of the Golden Dome architecture is being built on a single company's infrastructure.

sections.heading: Europe's Defense Buildup: EU Ambassador Says Delivering Is No Longer Just a Promise
sections.body:
EU Ambassador to the US Jovita Neliupšienė penned an op-ed in Defense One arguing that Europe's defense investment is translating from pledges into tangible output. Europe collectively spent 2.1% of GDP on defense in 2025, with frontline allies Poland, Lithuania, Latvia, and Estonia moving toward 5% of GDP. Eighteen member states have accessed the EU's new $200 billion defense financing program, with over $6 billion already deploying.

At the NATO Summit, Lockheed Martin and Rheinmetall announced plans to produce ATACMS missiles in Germany — the first time the system will be manufactured outside the US. NATO Secretary General Rutte said the alliance will have the capacity to produce 4 million artillery shells annually by next year, nearly twice the 2025 rate.

Neliupšienė also noted that Europeans remain the largest customers of the US defense industry, accounting for nearly 40% of US arms exports worth $130 billion, while more than half of European defense procurement still comes from US suppliers. The EU is preparing its European Security Strategy later this year to define the next phase of defense transformation.
sections.whyItMatters: The data points are starting to back up the rhetoric: ATACMS production in Germany, 4 million shells per year capacity, and $6 billion actually deploying. The question is whether the industrial base can sustain the pace — and whether US-EU defense trade friction will grow or shrink as Europe builds more of its own.

bottomLine: July 14-15 marked a significant inflection in European defense sovereignty: nine nations formally backed Ukraine's Freyja interceptor with a 12-month deployment target, NATO chose Saab over Boeing for its next AWACS fleet, and the EU's top envoy argued that European defense spending is now producing real output — not just promises. Meanwhile, the Pentagon's own industrial base problems were on full display: 155-mm shell production stuck at 36% of its target, a $469 million facility producing zero usable parts, and a CMMC cybersecurity program suspended because it was driving contractors out of the supply chain. The $87.6 billion Iran war supplemental underscores the scale of the munitions crisis — $97.3 billion for munitions alone across FY27 — while the Navy's carrier drone RFI and the Golden Fleet's expanding architecture point toward a force structure built for mass and attrition tolerance rather than exquisite platforms.
sources: Defense News, Defense One, SCMP, DoD Inspector General, NATO, EU Ambassador to the US, Forecast International, AP, White House supplemental request
